title: Video Broadcast — Live Webcam with Broadcast Overlays

pages.title: Overview
pages.body:
A live webcam feed is rendered on a full-frame Three.js plane and dressed with broadcast-style DOM overlays: a pulsing LIVE badge, a lower-third name card that slides in after 1.5 seconds, a scrolling ticker band at the bottom, and a corner branding watermark.

Navigate through the pages below to walk through each section of this file.

pages.title: Overlay Builder Functions
pages.body:
Four private helpers construct each overlay piece and append it to a shared transparent div that sits absolutely over the canvas.

· _buildLiveBadge creates the top-right badge with a round red dot that is animated in update()
· _buildCornerBranding stamps a faint ARTLAB watermark in the top-left
· _buildLowerThird builds the sliding name-and-title card, starting off-screen at translateX(-110%)
· _buildTicker assembles the bottom band: a fixed TOPICS chip and a scrolling text node whose content is doubled so the seam never shows

pages.title: Setup
pages.body:
setup() positions the camera for a snug 16:9 framing (FOV 60, z=9), gates on a user-gesture button required for getUserMedia, then opens the webcam and maps its texture onto a 16×9 PlaneGeometry. A single overlay div is created with pointer-events:none and all four builder helpers are called to populate it.

The elapsed time reference and animation state (ticker position, lower-third progress) are reset here so the example restarts cleanly if reloaded.

pages.title: Update — Lower-Third & Ticker
pages.body:
The LIVE dot opacity is driven by a 3.2 Hz sine so it breathes rather than blinks. The lower-third becomes visible after 1.5 s of elapsed time and then slides in over 0.6 s using a cubic ease-out curve applied as a CSS translateX percentage.

The ticker advances _tickerX by TICKER_SPEED × dt pixels per frame; when the offset exceeds the full text width the counter wraps to zero, producing a seamless loop because the text content was doubled during construction.

pages.title: Teardown
pages.body: teardown() stops the webcam stream and removes the overlay div in a single call, which also removes all child elements. All module-level references are nulled so garbage collection can reclaim them between example loads.
